---
title: "Euclid × SPT-3G CMB lensing"
occasion: SPT Summer Collaboration Meeting
description: Status report on the Euclid TR1 × CMB-lensing cross-correlation programme — footprints, blinding, data vectors, and the road to a likelihood
draft: true
author:
    name: Cail Daley
    affiliation: on behalf of the Euclid CMBX team
date: 2026-06-29
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"

output-file: index.html

format:
    revealjs:
        width:  1920
        height: 1080
        auto-stretch: false
        theme: [default, custom.scss]
        slide-number: true
        fig-align: center
        include-after-body: assets/no_footer_on_titleslide.html
        footer: Cail Daley | Euclid × SPT-3G CMB lensing | SPT Summer Collaboration | June 2026
        history: false
        hash : true
        pdf-separate-fragments : true
        pdf-max-pages-per-slide	 : 1
        template-partials:
            - title-slide.html
        center-title-slide: false
image: images/spt26_footprint.png

html-math-method:
    method: mathjax
    url: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-svg-full.js
include-in-header: assets/mathjax.html
---

## Status report: Euclid × CMB-lensing cross-correlations {.center}

A status report on the **Euclid MoU CMB-lensing × cross-correlation project** —
led by **Cail Daley & Margherita Lembo**.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Today:**
footprints & fields · blinding · data vectors ·
estimator consistency · SPT vs ACT · likelihood & bias plans ·
systematics · simulations
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Timeline:**

- Euclid cosmology products now scheduled **June 2027**
- DR1 freeze slipped from **Oct 2026** [CONFIRM]
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is a status report, not a results talk — the detection and amplitude work is
blinded and in progress, so I'm reporting where the Euclid × CMB-lensing
cross-correlation project stands rather than headlining a number.

It's the Euclid MoU project, jointly led by me and Margherita Lembo, with Louis
Legrand and the rest of the 3×2pt+κ team.

Quick roadmap of where I'll go: the footprints and how we define the fields; our
blinding strategy; the data vectors — all three crosses; estimator consistency;
SPT versus ACT; then the forward-looking pieces — likelihood and bias plans,
systematics, and simulations.

On timing — and this matters for how we plan the MoU paper relative to Euclid: the
Euclid cosmology products are now scheduled for June 2027. That's the public
release / publication date set at the May consortium meeting; DR1 products are
final March 2027. This has slipped — the original DR1 freeze deadline was the
Oct-2026 red line, already missed in projection, mostly from shear-calibration
slippage. [CONFIRM the exact prior date before presenting — dictation was unclear,
and I want to be precise that the slip is freeze-Oct-2026 → publication-June-2027,
not muddle the freeze and publication dates.] The upshot the SPT side cares about:
we don't have to wait for June 2027 to publish — a paper can move on its own
schedule as long as what we publish matches what's frozen in the SAS.
:::

## Euclid footprints and overlaps

```{=html}
<div class="r-stack">
  <img src="images/spt26_footprint_1_euclid.png" style="width:84%">
  <img src="images/spt26_footprint_2_spt.png" class="fragment" style="width:84%">
  <img src="images/spt26_footprint_3_act.png" class="fragment" style="width:84%">
</div>
```

## Getting blinding out of the way

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="46%"}
- **Parameter-shift blind** — [Muir et al. 2019](https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.05929)
    - same approach as SPT D1 lensing & Euclid 3×2pt
- **Hidden cosmology** $\theta_{\rm blind}$ — shift applied to the data

::: {style="font-size:0.62em"}
$$\hat{C}_b^{\rm blind} = \hat{C}_b^{\rm data} + \mathbf{B}_{b\ell}\big[\,C_\ell(\theta_{\rm blind}) - C_\ell(\theta_{\rm fid})\,\big]$$
:::

- **Theory:** internal (eDR1like)
- **CMBX likelihood:** plan to move toward [CLOE](https://euclid-cloe.readthedocs.io)
:::
::: {.column width="54%"}
![](images/spt26_blinding_cosmologies.png){width="98%"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
A quick word on blinding, since it sets what every later slide can and can't show.

We use a standard parameter-shift blind — the Muir et al. 2019 scheme, the same family the
SPT-3G D1 lensing blind and the Euclid 3×2pt blind both use. The recipe is the equation on the
left: pick a hidden cosmology θ_blind, compute the cross-spectra it predicts, take the
difference from the fiducial prediction, window it through the same bandpower operator the data
went through, and add that ΔCℓ to the data vector — and only the data. The theory curves stay
fiducial and the covariance is untouched, so every bandpower shape and every error-bar size you
see today is honest; only the absolute amplitude is hidden. It's additive in Cℓ, so unblinding
is just subtracting the same ΔCℓ.

The hidden cosmology is drawn from the public list on the right — a hundred cosmologies
scattered about two sigma around Planck-18 in the Ωm–S8 plane, S8 being the lensing amplitude
the cross is most sensitive to. You can see the cloud stretch along the S8 degeneracy. The list
itself is public and shareable; the only secret is which entry we picked. That selection is
sealed on the compute node — an OS-entropy integer hashed to an index — so neither I nor the
pipeline ever sees the realized cosmology, and all that's recorded is the commitment hash at the
bottom.

The practical consequence for today: this is a status report read entirely under the blind —
shapes, relative consistency, error-bar sizes — never the amplitude.

Two pointers on the bottom bullets. The theory we shift against is internal — the eDR1like
prediction from our own pipeline, not an external Boltzmann product. And the CMBX likelihood is
the eDR1like Gaussian likelihood today; the plan is to move it toward CLOE, the Euclid consortium
likelihood, so the cross slots into the official inference framework.
:::

## Data vectors: the three cross-correlations

![](images/spt26_cross_spectra.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}

::: {.notes}
This is the data-vector slide — what we measure, not how it fits.

The spine for SPT is the two CMB-lensing crosses, top and middle: shear × kappa and galaxy clustering × kappa, six tomographic bins each, NaMaster pseudo-Cℓ bandpowers on the joint Euclid × SPT mask.

The bottom row is the third cross — galaxy–galaxy lensing, delta_g × gamma, Euclid internal, no CMB lensing. One wrinkle to flag honestly: GGL isn't a single-field cross like the kappa-crosses, it's a six-by-six source(shear)-by-lens(clustering) matrix. The broad Euclid tomographic n(z) overlap means there's signal in essentially every pair, but the cleanest, highest-S/N configuration is "source behind the lens." So on the slide I show the deepest shear bin — bin six, which sits behind all the clustering bins — crossed with each clustering lens bin: one clean spectrum per panel, parallel to the rows above. The full six-by-six matrix is a backup figure (spt26_ggl_matrix.png) if anyone wants it — and you can see the source-behind triangle is where the signal lives.

I'm deliberately not quoting amplitudes or per-bin S/N here — those belong to the detection arc, which is blinded and out of scope for this status report, and I've stripped them from the figure so nothing leaks. The message of this slide is just: three crosses, one consistent estimator, consistent tomographic binning, all tracking the Planck-18 shape. The figure carries the weight.
:::

## Robust to the SPT-3G lensing estimator

![](images/spt26_estimator_robustness.png){width="66%" fig-align="center"}

[Highest-S/N bin (6): GMV vs the bias + profile-hardened reconstruction (and PP) agree to
**<1σ** per bandpower (pull RMS 0.28σ γ×κ, 0.29σ δ×κ), at no S/N cost — estimator-robust. A
foreground term *common* to all estimators cancels in the difference and is **not** tested
here (reserved Websky/Agora recovery).]{.credit style="font-size:0.55em"}

::: {.notes}
This is a status check on whether the cross depends on which SPT lensing estimator we
feed it. I'm showing the highest-S/N bin (bin 6), the two CMB-lensing crosses stacked
top and bottom — cosmic shear × κ and galaxy clustering × κ; the same picture holds across all
six bins. Each panel overlays three SPT-3G estimators — GMV, PP, and the bias +
profile-hardened reconstruction (GMVbhTTprf) — and they sit on top of each other.

The message is one line: swapping estimators moves every bandpower by less than one
sigma — pull RMS 0.28 sigma in gamma-kappa, 0.29 in delta_g-kappa, all 120 bandpowers
inside 1 sigma, and the detection S/N doesn't drop. So the measurement is
estimator-robust.

Be careful here, and I want to be honest with this audience because you know lensing
biases cold: this is NOT a foreground-clean certificate. A tSZ or CIB contribution that
is common to GMV and the hardened estimator cancels in the difference — the hardening
correction being small bounds the *part that differs between estimators*, not the total
foreground bias. The direct test is a Websky or Agora-with-HOD recovery, which is
reserved and not yet run. So: estimator-robust, hardening correction bounded below one
sigma — and that's the claim, no more.
:::

## Two κ surveys on the same Euclid bins: SPT-3G and ACT DR6

![](images/spt26_cross_survey.png){width="66%" fig-align="center"}

[Highest-S/N bin (6): **shear×κ** (top) and **clustering×κ** (bottom), Euclid TR1 crossed
against **SPT-3G GMV** (red ○) and **ACT DR6** (teal □) over the common fiducial theory.
Two independent κ reconstructions on the *same* southern bins.]{.credit style="font-size:0.55em"}

::: {.notes}
This is the cross-survey check. Same Euclid tracers, crossed against two independent
CMB-lensing reconstructions — SPT-3G GMV in red, ACT DR6 in teal — both laid over the
fiducial theory curve. I'm showing the highest-S/N bin (bin 6): shear×κ on top,
clustering×κ on the bottom; the same picture holds across all six bins.

The message is just: we have two κ surveys over this southern overlap, and we can put both
through the identical pipeline on the identical bins. I'm deliberately *not* making an
amplitude or consistency claim here — that arc is blinded and in progress. The transferable
point is that the cross is reproducible across reconstructions, and that gives us a lever:
where SPT constrains better we lean on SPT, where ACT does we lean on ACT.

[SECOND ANGLE — the next slide.] The direct "who constrains where" view: markers placed on
theory, only the error bars real, so the σ(ℓ) of each survey reads off directly — the exact
full Gaussian diagonal for both surveys. ACT is marginally tighter only at the largest scales
(the lowest 2–3 bandpowers, ℓ≲180); **SPT is tighter across the bulk of the range** (ℓ≳200),
by a σ ratio of ~1.5× through the mid-range and up to ~2.5× at ℓ≈2700 — its depth and
resolution win everywhere but the very largest scales.
:::

## Which κ survey constrains where

![](images/spt26_kappa_constraints.png){width="66%" fig-align="center"}

[Highest-S/N bin (6), **$\gamma\times\kappa$** (top) and **$\delta_g\times\kappa$** (bottom):
markers placed **on the fiducial theory** (no measured amplitude), each carrying its survey's
real per-bandpower error bar — the **exact full data-fiducial Gaussian** σ (TR1, log15) —
**SPT-3G GMV** (red ○) vs **ACT DR6** (teal □). Shaded = **where SPT's error bar is the smaller
of the two**: ACT marginally tighter only at the lowest 2–3 bandpowers (ℓ≲180), SPT tighter for
ℓ≳200 (σ ratio ∼1.5× mid-range, ∼2.5× by ℓ≈2700).]{.credit style="font-size:0.55em"}

::: {.notes}
This is the "who constrains where" view — it answers the lever question from the previous
slide. The markers are placed *exactly on the fiducial theory curve* — there's no measured
amplitude here — and each carries its survey's real per-bandpower error bar, σ from the
on-disk covariance. So the whole figure is about the *size* of the error bar, and it's
blinding-safe by construction. Both CMB-lensing crosses: shear×κ on top, clustering×κ on the
bottom — the picture is the same in both.

The story is a crossover around ℓ≈200, and it favors SPT almost everywhere. ACT DR6 is
marginally tighter — by under 10% — only at the very largest scales, the lowest two or three
bandpowers (ℓ ≲ 180), where its wider Euclid overlap gives it more modes. From ℓ≈200 on,
SPT-3G GMV is tighter: its error bar is smaller by a factor of about 1.5 through the mid-range,
growing to about 2.5 by ℓ≈2700, where its depth and angular resolution dominate. The shaded band marks the
SPT-tighter region. So the honest takeaway for this audience: across the bulk of the usable
range SPT is the better-constraining reconstruction on these bins, with ACT competitive only
at the very largest scales.

If asked about the covariance — and this audience will ask: these are the **exact full
data-fiducial Gaussian** per-bandpower σ — the covariance diagonal — for *both* surveys, now
that the full ACT Gaussian exists on disk alongside SPT's. No Knox approximation, no bound: I
slice the bin-6 κ-cross block straight out of each survey's 24-spectrum NaMaster Gaussian
covariance. The crossover sits at ℓ≈200 (γ×κ flips by ℓ≈176, δ_g×κ by ℓ≈221), and it is
*lower* than the Knox-diagonal figure that circulated earlier (ℓ≈250) for a physical reason:
the full Gaussian inflates ACT's low-ℓ variance more than SPT's — ACT's wider Euclid overlap
carries more mode-coupling correction relative to the f_sky-scaled Knox approximation. So the
exact result is, if anything, *more* favorable to SPT than the Knox story. (The ℓ≈1350 number
from much earlier was a different comparison — combined-tomography on the older SACC vector —
not this like-for-like bin-6 one.)
:::

## Likelihood — the plan

**Being built**

- A **joint Gaussian likelihood** over the cross data vector — covariance from theory now, mocks next.

::: {.notes}
This is the most forward-looking slide — much of the likelihood is still being designed, so read it as a plan, not a result.

What's being built: a joint Gaussian likelihood over the cross data vector. Right now the covariance is theory-based; the simulations slide that follows is partly in service of getting to a mock-based covariance.

How galaxy bias gets handled: we fit one amplitude A together with a per-tomographic-bin galaxy bias b_i — a vector {A, b_1, ..., b_6}. The structural point: adding the galaxy clustering auto-spectrum delta-g delta-g to the galaxy-lensing cross delta-g times kappa tightens each per-bin bias b_i by roughly a factor of two and a half — so delta-g delta-g genuinely adds bias-breaking power, pinning the b_i much better.

But — the honest limitation — that does NOT yet break the amplitude-bias degeneracy. In the cross-correlation A and b come in as a product, so even with delta-g delta-g sharpening the b_i, A and b stay degenerate along the same direction. No amplitude numbers here — that arc is blinded and dropped from this talk.

So the genuinely open design question, which I'd welcome thoughts on, is what additional information actually breaks A from b. Candidates: the shear-ratio geometry between tomographic bins, or pushing the delta-g delta-g scale range — but this is unsettled.
:::

## Systematics: galactic extinction is sub-σ in every bin

![](images/spt26_extinction_xell.png){width="92%" fig-align="center"}

[Galactic-extinction coherent bias $X_\ell = C_\ell^{\kappa S}C_\ell^{fS}/C_\ell^{SS}$
(DES/Chang) in units of the measurement error $\sigma$, per tomographic bin:
$\delta_g\times\kappa$ (left), $\gamma\times\kappa$ (right). **Sub-σ at every bandpower**
(worst $|X/\sigma|\!\approx\!0.46$); aggregate **0.35–0.8σ** per bin. One template is not a
systematics budget.]{.credit style="font-size:0.6em"}

::: {.notes}
This is a deliberately honest slide — I want to be clear about what we have and haven't done
on systematics, because it's the part of the analysis that's least advanced.

The one cross-correlation systematic we've actually quantified on this catalog is galactic
extinction. The metric is the standard DES/Chang contamination estimate: X_ℓ equals the cross
of kappa with the extinction template, times the cross of the tracer with extinction, divided
by the extinction auto — that's the coherent bias the template can inject into our signal. I'm
plotting that bias in units of the measurement error, sigma, as a function of ell, for each of
the six tomographic bins — clustering-cross-kappa on the left, shear-cross-kappa on the right.

The message reads straight off the y-axis: it's sub-sigma at every single bandpower — the worst
is about 0.46 sigma — and the aggregate per bin is between a third and eight tenths of a sigma.
So extinction is not a showstopper. The excursions are largest at the lowest ell, which is what
you'd expect for a smooth galactic template.

But the transferable message is the title's other half: this is one template. Stellar density,
exposure depth, zodiacal light, the north-south asymmetry — those null tests exist from an
earlier catalog version but have NOT been redone on the catalog this analysis runs on, so I'm
not quoting them. One template quantified does not close systematics — this is the most open
work-package in the analysis, and I'd flag it as such rather than imply we've got systematics
under control.
:::

## Simulations: mocks for covariance, foregrounds, and noise

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Covariance**

- **DEMNUni / FLASK** mocks → a mock-based covariance.

**Foregrounds**

- **Agora** sky simulation.
- **Marco Gatti** building Euclid-like products from Agora.
- A **systematics-injected GLASS box** within Euclid — *(same injection could go into the Agora Euclid mocks)*.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Noise realizations**

- **D1 mock lensing reconstruction** ($\hat\kappa - \kappa_{\rm true}$) as noise realizations for our mocks.

**Ambitious**

- Run the **D1 pipeline on Planck FFP10** lensing sims → properly estimate the **cross-covariance** between **Planck, ACT, and SPT**.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the simulations leg — where the mock program stands, organized by what each set of
mocks is *for*.

Covariance: we have DEMNUni and FLASK mocks, which give us a route to a mock-based covariance
to replace the theory covariance the likelihood uses now.

Foregrounds: the Agora sky simulation is the workhorse, and Marco Gatti is building
Euclid-like products from Agora that we can run through our pipeline. Separately there's a
systematics-injected GLASS box within Euclid — and the same systematics-injection idea could
be applied to the Agora Euclid mocks.

Noise: the plan is to use the D1 mock lensing reconstruction — reconstructed kappa minus true
kappa — as noise realizations for our mocks, so the reconstruction noise is realistic rather
than analytic.

And the ambitious one, which I'll flag as genuinely ambitious: I've been thinking about
running the D1 lensing pipeline on the Planck FFP10 lensing simulations, so we can properly
estimate the *cross*-covariance between Planck, ACT, and SPT — the off-diagonal blocks you'd
need to combine reconstructions rather than pick one.
:::

## Summary & next steps {.center}

**Where we are** — Euclid TR1 × SPT-3G, a status report:

- Maps & fields defined; a standalone self-blind in place.
- All three cross data vectors measured: $\delta_g\times\gamma$, $\delta_g\times\kappa$, $\gamma\times\kappa$.
- Estimator- and cross-survey-consistent (SPT-3G GMV vs profile-hardened; SPT vs ACT).

::: {.fragment}
**Next** — joint $\{A, b_i\}$ likelihood · mock-based covariance · close systematics · DR1.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Thanks — Euclid CMBX SWG: **Margherita Lembo, Louis Legrand, Giulio Fabbian** and the 3×2pt+κ team — and the **SPT-3G lensing** team, whose winter GMV $\kappa$ makes this cross possible.
:::

::: {.notes}
Quick wrap. Where we are: the maps and field definitions are built and a self-blind is in place, so the analysis is running on a fixed, blinded footprint. We have all three cross data vectors in hand — galaxy–galaxy lensing, density–kappa, and shear–kappa. And the crosses are consistent two ways: across the SPT lensing estimator — GMV versus profile-hardened — and across surveys, SPT versus ACT. That's the status: the measurement infrastructure is in place and internally consistent.

What's next, and this is where the real work is: build the joint per-bin {A, b_i} likelihood; move to a mock-based covariance; close out systematics properly — extinction is the only one redone so far; and then DR1. (The gamma-gamma shear auto-spectrum, which was flagged as a broken spin-2 product earlier in the analysis, is now restored — the failure was a stale purified MCM-workspace cache, not an estimator problem, fixed and regression-guarded — so it's no longer a gap; it gives us the shear-calibration lever for the joint fit.)

Deliberately no amplitude number on this slide — that arc is blinded and in progress, so I'm reporting status, not a result.

Thanks to the Euclid CMBX SWG — Margherita Lembo, Louis Legrand, Giulio Fabbian and the 3×2pt+κ team — and to the SPT-3G lensing team, whose winter GMV kappa maps make this whole cross possible. Happy to take questions.
:::
